one_liner: The pill imprint becomes the helpline number.
search_doc:
The pill imprint becomes the helpline number.
Mechanisms: everyday-object-transformation, data-as-content, intervention-at-point-of-use, purpose-driven-product
Idea: 855-HOW-TO-QUIT — a helpline that reaches people in the most critical moment, when they have a pill in their hand. It turns the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills into phone extensions: codeine (IP33) is dialled as 4733, oxycodone (K9) as 59. The caller then hears the story of someone who managed to quit that very pill, followed by live advice from experts.
Insight: The imprint code on a pill is read at the exact moment a user is about to take it — the closest possible touchpoint to a relapse decision.

full_record.file_path: 2024/MEDIA/BRONZE/855-How-To-Quit-(Opioids) - Anzen Health (Serviceplan Munich).jpeg

extracted.tagline: The helpline that turns the object of addiction into a way out.
extracted.context: Every 6 minutes, someone in the United States dies from opioids. However, opioids remain widely accessible for medical reasons. If we can't prevent people from getting their hands on these pills, how can we prevent them from abusing them?
extracted.idea: 855-HOW-TO-QUIT — a helpline that reaches people in the most critical moment, when they have a pill in their hand. It turns the mandatory imprint codes on opioid pills into phone extensions: codeine (IP33) is dialled as 4733, oxycodone (K9) as 59. The caller then hears the story of someone who managed to quit that very pill, followed by live advice from experts.
execution: Helpline that routes callers via opioid imprint codes (IP33→4733, K9→59)
Stories from people who quit that exact pill, then live expert advice
Nationwide integrated launch with contextual OOH, social and film
Coalition of NGOs, activists, artists and media companies
PSA on national TV and dedicated website
metrics: 6.1+ M Americans suffer from opioid use disorder

inferred.insight: The imprint code on a pill is read at the exact moment a user is about to take it — the closest possible touchpoint to a relapse decision.
inferred.one_liner: The pill imprint becomes the helpline number.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Recodes the imprint stamped on every opioid pill into a working helpline phone extension, intercepting users at the moment of relapse and generating 8,000+ calls and 137M contacts in four weeks.

visual.style_description: Clean editorial layout dominated by an outdoor billboard reading '855-HOW-TO-QUIT-(36-01)' with colourful pills replacing the parenthesis digits; left column carries dense problem/idea copy, bottom strip shows the integrated rollout (OOH, social, PSA, TV, web).

idea.rationale: The channel idea recodes the FDA-mandated imprint on every opioid pill into a working phone-extension number — IP33 becomes 4733, K9 becomes 59 — so the pill itself becomes the helpline's media buy. It is a clean everyday-object-transformation that lives at the exact relapse moment, with the pill in hand.

strategy.rationale: Anzen Health is a US healthcare consultancy positioning itself around addiction-recovery support, so a hotline anchored to opioid imprint codes is on-mission for a relatively young brand still building category credibility. The context '6.1M+ Americans suffer from opioid use disorder' and the 'every six minutes someone dies' framing are real, citable public-health numbers consistent with SAMHSA and CDC data. Mission-fit is solid, but a small-consultancy entrant on a national PSA scale leaves the strategic question of whether Anzen has the recovery capacity to receive the calls it generated — the board does not address it.

execution.rationale: Orchestration links the imprint-code mapping to a routed helpline that delivers a peer story for that exact pill followed by a live expert handoff, layered on a national integrated launch with contextual OOH, PSA broadcast TV, social and a coalition of NGOs and media companies. The single elegance is that the imprint codes do double duty as both targeting logic and intervention timing — the same number that triggers the call also identifies the substance.

impact.rationale: The 8,000+ calls and 142 recovery storytellers are countable behaviour-change inputs and are the right unit metrics for a helpline, though they are modest in absolute terms against a 6.1M opioid-use-disorder population. '137 million contacts in 4 weeks' is an entrant-reported reach claim that conflates exposure with intervention and should be read as a soft top-of-funnel number rather than evidence of recovery outcomes. The board carries no follow-up data on completed treatment, sustained abstinence or referral-conversion, which is the real business outcome for a recovery-positioning consultancy.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because recoding pill imprints into helpline extensions is a genuinely original channel insight, but a four-week reach number and 8,000 calls sit short of the recovery outcome a helpline would need to climb higher.

Idea: The clever bit: take over digital screens adjacent to McDonald's restaurants with hyper-contextual 'Aaand a…' creative — letting the surrounding city's media infrastructure perform the up-sell McDonald's staff already make every day.

extracted.insight: In a drive-thru queue, the menu board is the customer's most attentive screen — and 'Aaand a…' is the most natural up-sell moment in the McDonald's experience.
extracted.idea: The clever bit: take over digital screens adjacent to McDonald's restaurants with hyper-contextual 'Aaand a…' creative — letting the surrounding city's media infrastructure perform the up-sell McDonald's staff already make every day.

Cycled creative starting with the brand's own ad and ending with an 'Aaand a…' McDonald's menu-item teaser

inferred.one_liner: Other brands' billboards become the McDonald's up-sell.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned competitors' and partners' adjacent billboards into McDonald's up-sell teasers via a low-budget contextual OOH hack, demonstrated across multiple cities and partner brands.

visual.style_description: Before/after hero billboard split: a Pams ice-cream billboard ends with a yellow 'Aaand a…' Sundae cone teaser bolted to it, photographed against a dusk McDonald's forecourt; bottom strip shows seven more partner-board hacks (Lumo, Honda, Pams, Barkers etc.) to prove the system.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is hyper-local — every digital billboard within line-of-sight of a McDonald's restaurant is, in effect, prime up-sell inventory because customers approaching the drive-thru are already in the 'Aaand a…' decision mindset. Bolting a McDonald's menu-item teaser onto a neighbouring brand's billboard converts adjacent OOH into a contextual upsell engine — a clean contextual-OOH idea, though the mechanic is a media buy rather than a new format.

strategy.rationale: McDonald's New Zealand is the country's dominant QSR chain whose menu-board economics depend on the up-sell moment ('Aaand a…'), making the work mission-fit at the transactional level rather than borrowed purpose. The insight that 'Aaand a…' is the most natural up-sell language at the McDonald's counter and drive-thru is observationally credible, although the board does not anchor it in transaction data. The partnership choice (using existing OOH partner relationships rather than buying new inventory) is sharp but tactical rather than category-redefining.

execution.rationale: Hijacked digital billboards from Lumo, Pams, Honda and Barkers within line-of-sight of restaurants, cycling each board's own creative into an 'Aaand a…' McDonald's teaser as the closing frame. Hyper-local placement adjacent to physical stores is the channel-fit core, and the proof grid of seven partner-board executions demonstrates scale across the OOH network.

impact.rationale: No metrics are presented on the board — no sales lift, no incremental up-sell rate, no footfall measure. The business outcome a QSR chain cares about (transactions, average-basket-size lift, drive-thru add-on attach rate) is the obvious metric for an up-sell-targeted campaign and is conspicuously absent. The board carries no press coverage either, leaving the impact case unevidenced; the jury has to take the strategic logic on its own terms.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the contextual-OOH up-sell hack is on-brief and inventively placed within line-of-sight of stores, but the board carries no business metrics for an idea whose entire premise is incremental basket-size.

Idea: To get the potential urban adopters thinking, we had to innovate the livestream's distribution and implement it into every single digital billboard in cities around Finland.
Insight: In truth, shelter cats adapt well to home environments and make lovely pets. People just had to see how shelter cats are truly lovely — not in polished advertising, but in real life. extracted.idea: To get the potential urban adopters thinking, we had to innovate the livestream's distribution and implement it into every single digital billboard in cities around Finland.
execution: Utilised Finland's recently widespread 5G connections to push live shelter footage to digital DOOH
Hundreds of digital screens turned into live cat livestreams for two weeks
Each execution included a QR code, with which the viewer could instantly start the adoption process of the cat that wished to proceed to the home
Cat-shelter partner footage (live cat stream) supported by mobile, OOH and social

inferred.one_liner: The billboard becomes a live shelter window.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Plugged Finland's DOOH network into live shelter cams via 5G, lifting adoption applications by +147% and website traffic by +91%, plus double-digit brand-metric gains across awareness, preference and consideration.

visual.style_description: Hero photograph of a curious shelter cat behind a wire mesh, layered with a red 'ADOPTION LIVE' title block; right side shows a strip of DOOH placements with live-cat streams in city environments; bottom carries a six-metric ribbon with red percentage callouts and supporting case-footage thumbnails.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that polished shelter advertising fails to convey what shelter cats are actually like, so an unfiltered livestream from the shelter — pushed via 5G into Finland's full digital OOH network — converts every billboard into a live shelter window. The 'livestream-to-DOOH' idea is a clean Media reframe, though variations of live-feed billboards have appeared in cat-shelter work elsewhere, so the originality is more in the country-wide DOOH plumbing than in the conceptual move.

strategy.rationale: Musti Group is the Nordic region's largest pet-care retailer running this with SEY Animal Welfare Finland, the country's main animal-welfare NGO — so making shelter cats visible at urban scale is mission-fit for both partners rather than borrowed purpose. The context cites a verifiable Finnish reality (20,000 cats abandoned or born wild each year, shelters at capacity, harsh winters) and the insight that prejudice against wild-born cats blocks adoption is grounded in observable behaviour. The brand attribution between Musti and SEY is not foregrounded on the board, which makes the 'who is the brand' read slightly muddled.

execution.rationale: Orchestration leverages Finland's then-new nationwide 5G coverage to push live shelter feeds to hundreds of digital OOH screens for two weeks, with QR codes on each placement routing viewers straight into the cat's adoption flow. The choice to use DOOH as a continuous live medium rather than a slot-rotating ad surface is the right channel logic for an authenticity-first proposition, though the campaign relies heavily on existing DOOH inventory rather than building a bespoke placement type.

impact.rationale: +147% adoption applications and +91% website visitors are the right behaviour-change business outcomes for an adoption push, though both are unanchored — '+147%' versus what application baseline, '+91%' over what visitor period. 10M livestream impressions is entrant-reported and treated as directional reach. +31% awareness, +22% preference and +33% consideration are tracker-style brand-metric lifts presented without methodology, so they sit between claim and proof. No absolute number of actual adopted cats is shown, which is the cleanest outcome metric for an adoption campaign and is conspicuously absent.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because plugging live shelter cameras into Finland's national 5G-enabled DOOH grid is a credible single-medium-mastery idea, but the impact case rests on unanchored percentage lifts rather than absolute adoption counts.

inferred.one_liner: Your fine-dining receipt is a free Big Mac.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Hijacked Dubai's fine-dining receipts as media, driving a 43% footfall lift and 35% related sales increase by serving McDonald's combos to diners still hungry after their tasting menus.

visual.style_description: Dark moody board with golden M-arches stylised into an 'AFTER DINNER DINNER' wordmark; hero composition pairs an elegant restaurant receipt with a glossy Big Mac next to it; left and right text columns labelled Insight / Idea / Execution and a bottom row of phone screenshots and TV-press strip.

idea.rationale: The channel idea is to geo-target Snapchat and Instagram inside fine-dining restaurants at the moment small portions stop satisfying — a rival-context-hijack where the receipt becomes the currency. It is a clean trade-down play that turns competitor environments into McDonald's media.

strategy.rationale: McDonald's is the global QSR leader whose brand promise is reliable satiety at speed and value, so positioning the brand as the rescue meal after a small fine-dining portion is on-brand, not borrowed. The context 'Dubai has the most fine-dining restaurants per capita in the world' is an entrant-cited statistic that could be triangulated against industry indices but is not sourced on the board. The cited NYT-style 'still hungry after fine dining' insight is a genuine cultural observation but is asserted rather than backed by Dubai-specific data, and the [...] gap in the insight transcription weakens its strategic load-bearing.

execution.rationale: Orchestration links geofenced Snapchat and Instagram pushes inside fine-dining venues to a receipt-as-coupon redemption mechanic, with delivery couriers waiting outside the upmarket venues and POS integration on the McDonald's side. The single-medium discipline — using social geo-targeting tightly tied to a rival's premises — is the load-bearing channel decision, with influencer and TikTok amplification handled lightly.

impact.rationale: The 43% footfall and 35% related-sales lifts are the right business KPIs for a QSR activation but are unanchored — 43% of which baseline, in which restaurants, across what window. The 62M reach is entrant-reported and plausible for an MENA McDonald's push but not load-bearing for a sales-driven brief. The 15% brand-love lift is the soft signal, not the proof, and the press coverage (Forbes, Contagious, AdAge) is real but earned reach rather than commercial outcome.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because geofencing fine-dining venues to trade fine-dining receipts for Big Macs is a sharp rival-context hijack, but the impact metrics are unbased and the strategy carries a partial-citation insight.

Turning that signature into a Netflix trailer drop is a clean broadcast-ritual hijack that uses an unpaid moment of sports TV as the launch placement.

strategy.rationale: Netflix is the streaming category leader whose strategic challenge for any new docuseries is breaking through outside its own platform, and using Alcaraz's actual matches at Indian Wells to seed the launch is direct mission-fit because the talent IS the product. The insight that the camera-signing ritual is structurally uninterruptible across broadcasters is verifiable. The campaign rides Alcaraz's real-time competitive context rather than producing a paid trailer; however the board does not anchor the audience insight in any sports-viewership data and the strategic 'so what' beyond reach is light.

execution.rationale: Three matches at Indian Wells, three cryptic messages culminating in the reveal, broadcast live across 40+ channels in 160 countries, with Times Square OOH and social media revealing the documentary as the resolution. The orchestration uses three tournament moments as a serialised teaser, with the OOH and social acting as the payoff channels — channel-target fit is strong (tennis-broadcast viewers are also the docuseries audience).

impact.rationale: +700M earned reach and 20M live audience figures are entrant-reported broadcast claims, with the standard 'unaudited' caveat — Pattern 2 applies. The business outcome Netflix cares about — viewership lift, completion rate or sign-up data for the Alcaraz docuseries — is conspicuously absent (Pattern 3): a streamer should show tune-in, not impressions. The press wall (CNN, Guardian, ESPN, EL PAIS) is real but supports reach, not business return; the impact case rests on share-of-voice rather than proven tune-in.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because hijacking the post-match camera-signing ritual is a clean channel insight, but the impact case rests on share-of-voice with no tune-in or completion data — the real business outcome a streamer measures is absent.

one_liner: Dogs' stress becomes Lima's earthquake alarm.
search_doc:
Dogs' stress becomes Lima's earthquake alarm.
Mechanisms: data-as-media, animal-behaviour-as-sensor, geo-targeted-real-time-alerts, purpose-driven-product
Idea: PetPace partnered with London South Bank University to develop Animal Alerts — the first earthquake warning system powered by pet health data, using integrated media to alert residents in real time.
Insight: There is scientific evidence that animals change their behaviour even hours before an earthquake.

extracted.tagline: The first earthquake warning system powered by pet health data.
extracted.context: In 2023, 66,900 people died in earthquakes, many in Lima, Peru on the Pacific Ring of Fire where 90% of the world's seismic activity occurs. Today's technology still can't predict earthquakes — but what if animals could?
extracted.insight: There is scientific evidence that animals change their behaviour even hours before an earthquake.
extracted.idea: PetPace partnered with London South Bank University to develop Animal Alerts — the first earthquake warning system powered by pet health data, using integrated media to alert residents in real time.
execution: Smart collars on Lima dogs measure temperature, pulse and heart-rate variability to detect stress
Algorithm flags abnormal collective stress as a potential earthquake signal
Partnered with local pet stores; pet owners and dogs invited to join the program
Real-time alerts sent to Lima residents via geo-targeted mobile notifications, banners, digital OOH and radio

inferred.one_liner: Dogs' stress becomes Lima's earthquake alarm.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Turned pet biometrics into a city-wide early-warning system, alerting +9M Lima residents across 18 detected seismic events and earning broad earned-media coverage across Latin America and global tech press.

visual.style_description: Hero portrait of a black dog wearing a PetPace smart collar against a dark backdrop with concentric red seismic-wave rings; left column carries Background/Idea/Research blocks with small UI thumbnails, right column shows Technology/Execution/Results with a red Peru map and metric blocks; press logo strip at the bottom.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that pet-worn biometric sensors form a distributed, real-time animal-behaviour network that can be re-broadcast as a city-wide earthquake warning. Reframing a wearable pet-health product as urban seismic media is a category-defining, single-sentence Media idea — and crucially it is native to Media (sensor-as-channel, alert-as-placement) rather than a film with a tech veneer.

strategy.rationale: PetPace is a global pet-wearable brand making smart collars that monitor canine vitals, so converting its existing biometric stream into an earthquake-warning service is precise mission-fit with its 'pets help humans' positioning, not borrowed purpose. The work is grounded in concrete context — 66,900 earthquake deaths in 2023, Lima sitting on the Pacific Ring of Fire where 90% of global seismic activity occurs — and on a documented scientific insight that animals exhibit behavioural changes hours before tremors. The London South Bank University partnership lends academic credibility to a claim that would otherwise read as folklore.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs sensor-to-screen end-to-end: smart collars on Lima dogs feed temperature, pulse and heart-rate-variability data into an algorithm that flags collective stress, then triggers geo-targeted mobile push, digital OOH, banners and radio alerts in real time. Pet-store partnerships handle enrolment so the sensor network can scale on existing retail rails, and the multi-channel alert stack matches the moment-driven, location-bound nature of seismic warnings.

impact.rationale: The 18 detected seismic events and +9M residents alerted are the right business outcomes for an early-warning system — direct service delivery rather than surrogate impressions — and the unpaid pickup across Forbes, CNN, El País, Fast Company, Mashable, Telesur, Infobae and Peruvian national press is a credible breadth of earned conversation. The press logos are entrant-reported claims rather than audited reach, and the board does not disclose how many of the 18 alerts genuinely preceded real tremors versus false positives, which leaves the precision/recall of the system as an open question for a more skeptical jury.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because a pet-wearable brand turns its own product's biometric stream into a functioning city-wide earthquake-warning channel, making the media placement itself a public-safety utility rather than an ad about one.

one_liner: Every banned book becomes free where it is banned.
search_doc:
Every banned book becomes free where it is banned.

Idea: We decided to use the BMW iJack to ambush the company's EV journey on Google Maps. Presenting BMW iJack — turning Google Maps into the largest network of virtual showrooms by sending BMW electric cars to be photographed in advance every public EV charging station listed by visitors. But how do we do it? As the cars take the BMW Premium in the UAE, we arranged to dispatch the company of a gigantic effort to launch our electric BMW models to drive parked at every public charging point. Each electric BMW lives at every Google Maps EV-charging photoshoot map entry. So each time anyone (including would-be senior EV buyers) checks a Maps charging-stations entry, they get a BMW EV ready to act.
Insight: EV buyers' first instinct is to map charging stations — meaning Google Maps Street View is the moment a competitor's charger silently sells against you.

The use of Google Maps Street View's organic crawl as the distribution layer is the strategic unlock — paid media is replaced by an evergreen presence inside the platform's own search results.

execution.rationale: BMW EVs dispatched to every public charging station in the UAE ahead of Street View capture, with QR codes routing discoverers to iX/i7 booking pages — the orchestration depends on the precise timing of Google's mapping crawl, which is the load-bearing operational detail. The zero-budget claim is supported by the platform-native distribution, although the operational cost of physically positioning cars at every station is unaccounted for.

impact.rationale: The 400% increase in test drives is unanchored — 400% of what base, over what period, with what control? — and the '2 out of 5 new bookings from our virtual showrooms' is a credible attribution claim but rests on entrant tracking. 2M Google Maps views and $2.2M earned media are entrant-reported. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because parking a BMW at every UAE charging station before Street View captured it is a genuinely original platform-jailbreak, though the percentage-based impact claims are unbased and the jury read the work as tighter than the board's evidence supports.

inferred.qualitative_summary: When platforms blocked paid reach, US Senators, doctors and bestselling authors carried Dove's anti-toxic-beauty film organically — 11.1B earned impressions, 69M organic views, most-shared Dove film ever.

visual.style_description: Editorial half-and-half board: a soft-focus close-up portrait of a young girl fills the right half of the frame, while the left half holds the title in serif type, a row of small still-frames from the film, body copy and metric callouts; bottom band is a wall of press logos.

idea.rationale: The channel idea is to weaponise platform suppression itself: the same social platforms profiting from toxic beauty content moderate against work that exposes them, so distribution must run through high-credibility human amplifiers instead of paid reach. The platform-as-obstacle reframe is genuine but the resulting tactic — earned distribution by elites — is a familiar PR move rather than a category-defining channel mechanic.

strategy.rationale: Dove is Unilever's mass-market personal-care brand whose 20-year Real Beauty platform is the canonical example of a beauty brand built on body-confidence advocacy, so a film exposing social media's toxic-beauty harm is exact mission-fit, not borrowed purpose. The strategic premise — '3 in 5 kids suffering from mental health issues' — is a specific public-health-style number though its source is not cited on the board. The platform-suppression angle is plausible and consistent with broader reporting on algorithmic moderation, but the board does not show concrete documentation of the bans themselves.

execution.rationale: Orchestration is unusually thin for a Media entry: a long-form film distributed by US Senators, bestselling authors, doctors and professors, with audience-driven sharing replacing the blocked paid reach. There is no signature media-owner partnership, no real-time data layer, no channel format invention — the work succeeds as PR and earned-media propagation more than as a media-strategy mechanic, which is the native-to-category soft spot.

impact.rationale: The '+11.1B earned impressions' and '69M organic views' are entrant-reported reach figures plausible for a major Dove film but unaudited; the 'most-shared Dove film of all time' is a self-referential superlative against an internal benchmark. The board does not show downstream behaviour outcomes — sign-ups to Dove's Self-Esteem Project, policy traction, sales lift — which are the business outcomes a purpose brand's Media case would need to clear a higher band. The breadth of named press (NBC, NYT, HuffPost, Vogue, Allure, Daily Mail) is a credible signal of earned conversation, even if the impressions number is the load-bearing claim.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because Dove's purpose mission carries the entry but the case is essentially a film amplified by elites — a strong PR mechanic that doesn't fully earn a Media-native channel idea.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is genuinely new: the long-press shortcut menu on a phone icon — the moment of maximum attention before deletion — is a hidden, free media slot inside iOS and Android. Turning the OS gesture itself into a retention channel is a clean platform-jailbreak idea native to mobile.

strategy.rationale: Wendy's is the US burger chain whose brand voice is built on direct, slightly cheeky 1-to-1 interaction, so an idea that intercepts the customer's most decisive private moment with the app is on-voice rather than borrowed. The market focus is data-anchored: Argentina is named as the world's #1 app-deletion market at 51.4%, a specific statistic that locates the problem and justifies the local launch. Mission-fit is solid for a chain that lives in mobile ordering — though the Argentina pilot reads as a market test for a broader US play, which the board does not fully argue.

execution.rationale: Orchestration sits inside one channel — the OS shortcut menu — with personalised offers refreshed weekly based on order history and direct add-to-basket from the haptic menu. Single-channel mastery is exactly what Media rewards when the channel choice itself is the idea, though the absence of any earned-press partnership keeps the orchestration narrow.

impact.rationale: The four headline metrics — '+138% app usage, +12% sales, -14% drop rate, +237% social mentions' — are all percentages without an absolute base disclosed on the board, so each one is a directional claim rather than a hard outcome. The drop-rate cut is the right business KPI for an anti-deletion mechanic and is the most credible of the four; the sales lift is the right commercial outcome but is unanchored to a baseline order volume. The board carries no press partnerships to triangulate the user-side numbers.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the long-press shortcut menu is a genuinely original channel jailbreak, but the impact case is four unanchored percentages on a market-pilot footprint.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that harassment of women happens inside games, but the existing reporting channel (real-world police stations) is physically and procedurally disconnected from the moment of the offense. Building the police station as in-game architecture inside the platforms where the crime occurs is media-as-product thinking, with the media (the game environment) becoming the public service.

strategy.rationale: Wonder Women Tech is a Brazilian NGO advocating for women in gaming and tech, and the campaign sits exactly on its mission — converting in-platform harassment into actionable real-world investigations. The insight that Brazil ranks fourth globally for online crimes against women and that gaming platforms lack a real reporting channel is a cited statistical anchor, though the source is unstated. The institutional partnership with the Brazilian Civil Police is the strategic unlock that converts in-game reports into legally valid complaints — that legal equivalence is the load-bearing strategic claim.

execution.rationale: The build sits inside Fortnite, GTA V, Minecraft and Free Fire — the four highest-reach titles for the target audience — with NPC officers walking players through reports that auto-forward to the Civil Police's real system. The 18 implementations and platform-wide rollout demonstrate scale across the right channels for the audience, with PR-led awareness in Brazilian and international outlets layered on top.

impact.rationale: The 357 active investigations is the right business outcome (real-world legal consequence rather than impression count) and is the load-bearing proof; 25MM earned media and 91% positive mentions are entrant-reported claims, and 48% NGAR interaction is an unexplained acronym that limits how the jury can read it. The behaviour-change number is small in absolute terms relative to Brazil's gaming population, but in 'real investigations' rather than 'reported impressions' it carries unusual weight for a purpose campaign.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because building the police station inside the games where harassment happens is a credible media-as-product reframe with a real Civil Police partnership, but the business-outcome scale (357 investigations) is modest against the platform reach claimed.

one_liner: Every Montreal goal becomes a Budweiser ad.
search_doc:
Every Montreal goal becomes a Budweiser ad.
Mechanisms: name-as-content, language-pun-as-media, live-sports-hijack, rival-context-jailbreak
Idea: To get our crown back, we hijacked the only thing Molson couldn't put their name on: each and every goal scored by their own team. In French, 'Buuuuuuuud' means 'Goaaaaaal'. So by simply adding a few 'u's to our brand name, we made a giant inception ad at hockey fans in Quebec, making them think of our brand whenever their team was scoring a goal.
Insight: In Quebec hockey, the loudest moment of every match is the crowd yelling 'Buuuut!' — so stretching Budweiser's name into that exact sound makes every goal a Budweiser ad.

full_record.file_path: 2024/MEDIA/BRONZE/Et Le Buuuuud - Budweiser (BETC, Paris).webp

extracted.tagline: How Budweiser won Quebec back from Molson Brewery by hijacking hockey goals!
extracted.context: Budweiser is one of the top-selling beer brands in the world. But when it comes to Canada and its French-speaking province of Quebec, Bud is under pressure. The reason? People adore the city's hockey team, the Montreal Canadiens. An entire team owned by our competitor Molson Brewery, so that their brand could be associated with everything hockey-related.
extracted.idea: To get our crown back, we hijacked the only thing Molson couldn't put their name on: each and every goal scored by their own team. In French, 'Buuuuuuuud' means 'Goaaaaaal'. So by simply adding a few 'u's to our brand name, we made a giant inception ad at hockey fans in Quebec, making them think of our brand whenever their team was scoring a goal.
execution: Sponsored a few 'u's into the Budweiser brand name to mirror the French 'Buuuuuuuud' goal call
During Montreal Canadiens live banner displays inside the venue with 'Buuuuuuuud' visible across screens
Giant inception OOH across rail (metro) lines in Quebec, swapped all Budweiser signs and merch to hundreds of pubs, while introducing millions of Buuuuuuuud bottles, cans and packs over the franchise
Supermarket shelves turning existing consumers into our ambassadors
Used the phrase 'Buuuuuuuud' for the past 30 years; hockey fans' conversations
metrics: 15 million impressions (with only 8 millions inhabitants in Quebec)
Most mentioned brand during hockey matches online

inferred.insight: In Quebec hockey, the loudest moment of every match is the crowd yelling 'Buuuut!' — so stretching Budweiser's name into that exact sound makes every goal a Budweiser ad.
inferred.one_liner: Every Montreal goal becomes a Budweiser ad.
inferred.qualitative_summary: Stretched the Budweiser name to mimic the French goal cheer, hijacking the Canadiens' on-ice moments owned by Molson; +34.2% brand preference in Quebec, 15M impressions and #1 share in proximity stores.

visual.style_description: Hero composite of a Montreal Canadiens player skating beside a giant red Budweiser can re-labelled 'Et le Buuuuuud'; right side carries a grid of arena tunnel, supermarket aisle, OOH and bottle close-ups dressed in the stretched name; red banner-style title at the top and a five-metric red ribbon at the bottom.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is a linguistic-coincidence hijack: in Quebec hockey culture the goal cheer is 'Buuuuut!' which phonetically equals an elongated 'Buuuuuuuud' — letting the brand attach itself to every goal scored by a rival-owned team. Name-as-content tied to the loudest moment of every hockey match is a clean live-sports-hijack using a free media moment that the rival sponsor (Molson, who own the team) cannot pre-empt.

strategy.rationale: Budweiser is AB InBev's flagship global lager whose Quebec problem is that Molson Brewery owns the Montreal Canadiens and therefore owns 'hockey beer' by default — the strategic objective is to break that ownership without owning the team. The insight is exact: the goal cheer is the one moment in a hockey match where the rival sponsor cannot pre-empt the brand's audio-visual presence. The cultural scope is Francophone-Quebec only — the 'but/Bud' homophone does not transfer to Anglophone Canada — and the campaign owns that scope deliberately, treating regionality as the strategy rather than a limitation.

execution.rationale: The execution rolls across the whole Quebec ecosystem at once: arena banners during live games, metro OOH, pub merchandising, bottle and can re-labelling, and supermarket shelf takeovers — a region-saturating physical media buy timed to the Canadiens broadcast schedule. The phonetic stretch lives across every Budweiser owned and earned surface in the province, which is the orchestration core.

impact.rationale: The +34.2% brand preference lift is the right business outcome for a brand-share fight and the load-bearing number, although '+34.2% of what base?' is open and the measurement methodology isn't disclosed. The #1 beer in proximity stores and 98% supermarket distribution claims are credible category-share signals. 15M impressions against a market of 8M Quebec inhabitants is anchored by the disclosed population base — Pattern 1 satisfied — although the impressions number is entrant-reported. The Francophone-Quebec wordplay limits global jury readability (Pattern 5), which the impact case implicitly acknowledges by framing all numbers as Quebec-scoped.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the Buuuuud/But homophone is a Francophone-Quebec-only wordplay that works precisely because of its regional specificity — clever and culturally exact, but the joke is regional rather than pan-Canadian and the metrics, while category-relevant, rest on entrant-reported baselines.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that amateur Argentine sport keeps collapsing because a single missing player cancels the match, so a peer-to-peer app that pairs short-handed teams with available players becomes a piece of brand utility — Gatorade as the connector that keeps the game alive. The idea is a competent media-as-product move but the originality is modest: peer-to-peer matchmaking apps have existed for years across sport categories, and the work imports a familiar mechanic rather than inventing a new channel.

strategy.rationale: Gatorade is PepsiCo's global sports-fuel brand whose mission has long sat at the side of competitive athletes, so the strategic shift into amateur weekend players is a deliberate audience-expansion move that needs a credible reason for the brand to be there. The context is grounded in a verifiable Argentine reality (27 million weekly amateur players), and 'a missing player cancels the game' is a true everyday friction. The board's articulation of the insight is partially unreadable and the bridge from pro to amateur is asserted rather than backed by behaviour data on amateur Argentine sports culture.

execution.rationale: Orchestration is app-centric: a free Gatorade Match Saver app with filters for sport, time, location and distance, push notifications matching teams and players, plus influencer and athlete seeding content across LATAM and earned PR. Channel selection (utility app plus social plus athlete partnerships) matches the audience habit of organising weekend matches on phone, though the campaign is dominated by a single owned channel rather than a layered multi-channel media architecture native to a Media Lion.

impact.rationale: 21,765 new app users and 17,560 minutes played are the right behaviour-change outcomes for a utility play — and importantly they are absolute numbers, not percentages — though they are modest in a 27-million-amateur-player market (well under 0.1% penetration). 110M social impressions is entrant-reported reach treated as directional, and 96% positive sentiment is presented without methodology and reads as cherry-picked. No sales lift or Gatorade-product business outcome is shown — the obvious commercial KPI for a sports-drink brand is conspicuously absent.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because turning Gatorade into a peer-to-peer app that saves amateur matches is an on-brief utility move, but the impact case is small relative to the addressable amateur-sport market and the brand business outcome is missing.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that Brazilian viewers tune out the ad break but never the show, so the brand's own logo — the handshake — can be planted inside Globo's editorial programming as a discoverable cue. Recasting every on-screen handshake into a redeemable voucher trigger fuses brand iconography with broadcast content, which is a clean Media idea even if 'logo-as-trigger' has a small precedent in real-time-redemption history.

strategy.rationale: Mercado Libre is Latin America's largest e-commerce platform whose handshake logo encodes its founding promise of a 'good deal' between buyer and seller, so converting that exact icon into the Black Friday discount mechanic is precise mission-fit, not borrowed equity. The context — competing for attention against ad-break clutter on Brazil's #1 broadcaster — is plainly true of Brazilian TV's commercial environment, and Globo as partner is the only network with the editorial reach to make the cue national. The board doesn't cite hard data on TV ad-break attention loss, but the underlying behaviour is well-documented enough to read as credible rather than speculative.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs broadcaster-deep: deals embedded inside Globo programming rather than around it, QR overlays appearing in real time when handshakes occur on screen, and news/entertainment editorial coverage extending the mechanic across the network's day-parts. The partnership unlock with Brazil's dominant broadcaster is the structural lever — neither Mercado Libre nor Globo alone could turn an editorial gesture into a redemption trigger without that bilateral deal.

impact.rationale: The +80% sales lift is the right business outcome for a Black Friday e-commerce push and the strongest credible signal — though it is unanchored (+80% versus prior Black Friday? versus an average sales week?), so the base remains open. The 50M vouchers redeemed is a useful execution metric tying audience action to brand transaction. 7.8B impacts is entrant-reported and at the upper end of Brazilian-TV reach claims for a campaign window, treated as directional rather than verified. Press pickup across Veja, Estadão, Folha, G1, CNN and Globo is a credible breadth of earned conversation.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the brand's own logo is converted into a redemption trigger embedded inside the country's #1 broadcaster's programming — a partnership unlock no rival could replicate.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that Chinese New Year is the noisiest advertising window of the year in China and only a film release — not an ad campaign — can structurally break through. The insight that Chinese New Year ad space is saturated and a film-release framing is the only way to break through is a credible category claim. The board does not anchor the insight in CNY ad-spend or attention data, but the structural truth that CNY is China's loudest media moment is accepted category knowledge. The use of Tencent and movie-streaming placements is a coherent channel-target match for entertainment-seeking CNY audiences.

execution.rationale: Theatrical-style launch on Tencent and movie-streaming platforms, with editorial recommendation placements alongside genuine CNY blockbusters, cinema-marquee DOOH, news-headline takeovers and behind-the-scenes director spotlights — the entire orchestration borrows the marketing playbook of a film release rather than a brand. impact.rationale: The 498M reach (a third of China) and 7x-the-biggest-blockbuster views are entrant-reported scale claims and Pattern 2 applies to the unaudited surface metrics. 67% lifted brand perception is unbased ('67% of what survey?'); the 15.7M / 228M numbers are presented without labels that allow the jury to read them. 10'21" average view time is a credible engagement metric. The business outcome an iPhone launch ultimately cares about (purchase intent for the camera-led handset, share movement against Huawei) is referenced via 'lifted purchase intent' but not quantified — Pattern 3: the real outcome is implied rather than shown.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the work releases an iPhone product demo as a Chinese New Year film premiere with strong channel-target fit, but the impact case rests on large surface metrics whose bases are unclear and the camera-led purchase outcome is implied rather than measured.

inferred.one_liner: The sidewalk becomes the map.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that the sidewalk itself is an untapped tactile medium for visually impaired pedestrians — by extending the existing universal tactile-paving vocabulary, the pavement becomes a readable venue directory. Turning the brand's own raw material (cement) into a permanent accessibility interface is a clean media-as-product idea: the placement is the product is the message.

strategy.rationale: Sol is Peru's leading cement brand under UNACEM, whose mission centres on building national infrastructure — so turning its own product into a public accessibility system in Lima is exact mission-fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context is grounded in a verifiable reality of tactile-paving gaps (existing systems guide walking but offer no venue identification), and the system is co-developed with the largest Peruvian organisations for the visually impaired, which lends practical credibility. Open-sourcing the vocabulary for global adoption signals a category-leadership ambition coherent with the brand's national-builder identity.

execution.rationale: Orchestration is infrastructure-first: 75,000 m² of new tactile paving installed across Lima's Miraflores district, paired with user-training sessions so the visually impaired can actually read the extended vocabulary, then released open-source so other cities can replicate. The choice of Miraflores as pilot district and the partnership with the largest Peruvian accessibility organisations matches the audience exactly — a permanent in-environment channel rather than a stunt placement.

impact.rationale: The 500,000-people-benefited figure is the right behaviour-change outcome for accessibility infrastructure, though the board does not disclose how that population number is constructed (residents within the 75,000 m² zone? cumulative users?), so the absolute base is partly inferable but not explicit. The 75,000 m² installed is an unambiguous, audited execution metric and a durable asset rather than a campaign-window figure. Earned pickup across El Comercio, La República, TV Perú, designboom and Design Indaba signals genuine design-press conversation, but the board lacks a measured adoption-by-cities figure that would harden the open-source claim.

why_it_won.verdict: Wins Gold because a cement brand turns its core product into permanent accessibility infrastructure — the sidewalk literally becomes a readable venue map for the visually impaired.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that the same programmatic ad infrastructure built to find buyers can be repointed to find witnesses — same audience-targeting, same geo-precision, same dynamic-creative engine, but redirected to public-safety outcomes. 'Programmatic-as-witness-network' is a clean platform-jailbreak Media idea that takes a commercial channel and repurposes it as civic infrastructure.

strategy.rationale: The Dutch Police Force (Politie) is the Netherlands' national law-enforcement service whose mission depends on citizen cooperation, so an under-resourced force using ad-tech to reach witnesses at scale is direct operational fit rather than borrowed purpose. The context is grounded in two verifiable Dutch realities (20% victimisation rate, force understaffing, growing unsolved caseload), and the bridge from those frictions to programmatic witness-recruitment is logical. The board's articulation of the idea is partially garbled and over-jargoned, which makes the strategic 'why this brand can credibly run this channel' feel less than fully argued.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs as an operational system: programmatic ad-buying targeted by time and location of suspected crimes, dynamic creative built from live case data (witness asks, suspect descriptions), 'Witnesses Wanted' display ads launched within seconds of case alerts, and witness leads routed back into police case-management. The choice to use programmatic display rather than a dedicated witness-app is structurally right — it meets citizens where they already are rather than asking them to download anything.

impact.rationale: 500 cases solved and 7,500 cases leveraged are absolute, public-safety business outcomes — the right KPI for a police-force application of media — and arguably the cleanest impact figures in this batch. 450k clicks on assets is a useful engagement metric, and 50M impressions is treated as directional reach. The board does not disclose what proportion of those 7,500 cases yielded actionable tips versus those solved by other means, so attribution between programmatic-driven witnesses and standard police work is implicit rather than evidenced. The single press quote is a partner testimonial, not independent media verification.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because repointing programmatic ad infrastructure into a real-time witness-recruitment network for the Dutch Police is a credible platform-jailbreak, with absolute cases-solved outcomes that anchor the impact case better than most Media Bronzes.

idea.rationale: The channel idea is to turn a public backlash into the campaign itself — a formal apology that becomes 47 audience-tailored ASMR replacements for the offending slurp, served via interest-based targeting. It is a clean crisis-as-content play with personalisation as the channel mechanic.

strategy.rationale: Pot Noodle is Unilever's UK instant-noodle leader whose decades-long voice trades on cheeky, near-offensive humour, so absorbing a slurping-noise backlash and turning it into 47 satisfying-sound replacements is on-voice rather than off-mission. The competitive context is named — authentic overseas brands and private-label undercutting on price — but is asserted rather than data-anchored on the board. Mission-fit holds, though the strategy reads as a reactive crisis pivot retrofitted into a media-targeting play rather than an upstream channel strategy.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs as a press apology across UK national titles followed by 47 micro-targeted ad versions matching audience interest profiles (purring cats, ASMR triggers etc.) to alternative sounds, plus broad social and broadcast amplification. The ad-version personalisation is the load-bearing channel discipline; the press-apology placements are the bridge that re-injects the work into earned media.

impact.rationale: The 25% sales uplift in four weeks and the 19M pots sold figure are the right business KPIs for a fast-moving CPG, though the board does not disclose whether 19M pots is a campaign window or annual baseline volume. The '+399% online conversation' is an unanchored percentage typical of buzz tracking. The press logo wall (Sky News, BBC, The Sun, Mirror, Mail) is a credible signal of earned-media depth across UK nationals, lending the conversation claim more weight than a typical buzz metric.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because the apology-as-targeting pivot is a clever crisis-as-content play with a real sales uplift, but the channel mechanic is essentially audience-tailored advertising on the back of a brand-driven controversy.

The developer easter-egg lands on Google Cloud's real audience (developers in the console), making the channel-target fit precise. execution.rationale: Full-screen Sphere takeovers timed to the evening dates of AWS re:Invent, with the $gcloud unlock sphere easter-egg in the Google Cloud console as the developer-only handshake, and the unlocked promo code driving discounted Google Cloud Next '24 tickets. impact.rationale: 2,200+ command-prompt activations is the right business outcome for a developer-targeted activation and is anchored as an absolute number rather than a percentage. 550+ minutes of airtime, 38,906 earned engagements and 588,219 impressions are credible absolute figures (not percentages), but the absence of press-coverage logos and the small total impression number relative to a major spectacle suggests the cultural reach was narrower than the Sphere format implies. why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because owning the Las Vegas Sphere on AWS re:Invent week is a sharp rival-context hijack with a developer-precise easter-egg conversion layer, but the impact numbers are modest absolute counts that don't close the loop to enterprise-cloud business outcomes.

idea.rationale: The channel idea is to use the German media system itself as a single shaking screen — every TV channel, OOH panel, radio station and web banner triggering the quake animation at the exact same second. The medium becomes the message: the country feels what Turkey-Syria felt, on the anniversary, on the screens it cannot ignore.

strategy.rationale: Aktion Deutschland Hilft is the German alliance of leading humanitarian aid organisations (Caritas, World Vision, Malteser and others) coordinating disaster fundraising — using the country's entire media estate as a shared shaking siren is exact mission-fit for an alliance whose value proposition is national coordination. The board's strategic premise — that donations collapse weeks after a catastrophe even when need persists — is a well-documented behavioural pattern in humanitarian fundraising and is treated as a given rather than data-cited. strategy.rationale: IKEA is the Swedish global home-furnishings retailer that opened its largest physical store anywhere in the world in the Philippines two years prior, so the brand's strategic problem is converting that flagship's reach into nationwide online conversion — making 'every IKEA product in the wild is the store' a direct extension of the brand's accessibility mission. The 13th-month launch ties the timing to a recognised Filipino retail moment (year-end bonuses). The board provides no hard data on Filipino online-shopping behaviour or IKEA's pre-campaign baseline penetration, so the insight reads as observational rather than evidenced.

execution.rationale: Orchestration runs ambient-to-digital: staged real-life scenes featuring IKEA's signature yellow umbrella, beach chairs and tents in everyday Filipino settings, each carrying a QR linked to the IKEA online store, then amplified through social-media collages and CNN earned pickup. The channel selection (ambient stunts plus social plus owned e-commerce) matches the conversion intent, though the craft quality reads as medium and the executions function more as photographed scenes than as fully-engineered media placements.

impact.rationale: 4.5x online traffic, 30% online transactions and 11% online revenue are the right e-commerce business outcomes for a flagship-to-online conversion play, though all three are unanchored — '4.5x' versus what baseline, '30%' of which transaction set, '11% revenue' lift on what comparison period. The CNN headline 'IKEA is now EVERYWHERE!' is a single earned-press signal rather than broad cultural pickup, and the absolute figures (visit volumes, peso revenue) are not disclosed, so the case is directionally credible but quantitatively thin against a Silver bar.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Silver because the brand turns every IKEA product in everyday Filipino life into a shoppable storefront — a tidy media-as-storefront idea executed with breadth, even if the impact metrics float without absolute bases.

inferred.qualitative_summary: Pop-Tarts turned a Netflix biopic into a co-marketing engine by staging a public feud with Jerry Seinfeld, riding the launch to 297M impressions and a $115M incremental sales lift in two weeks.

visual.style_description: Editorial collage with side portraits of Jerry Seinfeld (smiling left, scowling right) framing five black-and-white film stills labelled 'Pop-Tarts took Jerry's characters / show / career / spotlight / props'; quote bubbles top and bottom, metric block centre-bottom.

idea.rationale: The channel insight is that a Netflix biopic about Pop-Tarts was already going to drive cultural conversation around the brand whether or not Pop-Tarts engaged, so reframing it as a fake feud (Jerry 'stole' the brand and the brand 'steals' Jerry back) turns the film's entire press tour into Pop-Tarts media for free. Movie-promo-hijack via fake-feud-narrative is a clean partnership-unlock with Netflix, though the mechanic is well-trodden brand-as-character territory.

strategy.rationale: Pop-Tarts is Kellanova's nostalgic American toaster-pastry brand whose strategic problem on launch weekend was either being defined by a film it didn't make or co-opting that narrative on its own terms. The Netflix Marketing Partnership unlock is the strategic core — the work only exists because the film studio agreed to co-orchestrate the feud, making this a genuine partnership rather than a parasitic ambush. The insight that brands can ride a movie's launch by playing the aggrieved counter-party is credible but not novel. The board does not anchor the insight in audience-attitude data; the strategic claim is intuitive rather than evidenced.

execution.rationale: Pop-Tarts 'stole' Jerry's characters in branded social posts, his show in TV appearances, his career in spoof ads, his red-carpet spotlight, and his movie props in merchandise — a five-act narrative timed to the film's press tour. The orchestration runs across paid social, earned TV, OOH and retail with each beat re-using the film's own promotion cycle as the free media wave.

impact.rationale: The +$115M incremental sales within 2 weeks is the right business outcome for a CPG brand and is a load-bearing absolute number (not a percentage) — Pattern 1 satisfied; the +57.6% incremental sales vs. year-ago is anchored to an absolute equivalent. 297M impressions and 17,000 sweepstakes entries are credible mixed surface/behavioural metrics. The +425% vs. TikTok benchmark is unbased ('of what benchmark?'). The #1 Netflix movie on launch weekend credit is for the film, not the brand. The business-outcome rigour is unusually strong for a media board.

why_it_won.verdict: Lands Bronze because turning a Netflix biopic's press tour into a fake feud is a clean Netflix-partnership unlock with rare business-outcome rigour ($115M incremental sales as an absolute number), though the fake-feud mechanic is conventional and the underlying channel insight is more tactical than transformational.
